speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please continue your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --when they-- when they surrounded the-- the-- the bells-- the bell-ringer you know, we didn't know where to go no more. So I gave Indja Benderski an idea. Maybe we'll go back to that farm where I used to work. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And maybe I find over there-- because I had lots of friends, I used to work for them, for the horses, for this and that, you know. Maybe we'll go there. I say, OK. So we went from Krzemieniec back to Tetylkowce to find a hiding for us. So we came there. We came to a guy. I know him very good. He was a shoemaker. He said, Benny, [NON-ENGLISH], you go away from here. Ukraine alone, they will kill you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a guy, was the bookkeeper from that farm. I went into his house. And I ask him, what kind of deal he has for me. I know-- he was a Pollack. He wasn't a Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, the only thing you can do, you run. You find a way to go east, northeast. Over there is all kind towns. And over there must be partisan.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I took that advice. He gave me some advises. How I can find the north and the south and east and west. If you come to a street, to the forest, you'll see the tree growth. And here is the growth here. And this side you ever will know that's the north. If this is the north, you will see where the south is.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I find this. I said, very good advice.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, right away, in the same night, somebody find out that we are on the farm. And they tried to catch us. And they didn't catch us. We run away from Tetylkowce to Dubno. That was, I would say, about 70 mile-- 70 kilometer.

speaker: interviewee
text: In one night, we couldn't make it. Tonight, we could walk. We could move only the nighttime. In the daytime it's impossible. So we made about 30 or 35 kilometer to other woods, from one place to other place. And then, in the next night, we went from that place to Dubno. In Dubno, we came in the ghetto. That was a ghetto. They didn't know what happened in Krzemieniec.

speaker: interviewer
text: I say, what's the matter? They try to dig some ditches in the bottom. They think the Germans, they will go away right away. That wasn't like that. In Dubno we find out that little bit northeast of Dubno, over there is a little town, Ostrozac.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ostrozac. I say, if you go there to Ostrozac, you'll find out, people-- they're going to-- that was a Polish organization-- they try to make a partisans. So we tried to be the partisans. So went in the nighttime to Ostrozac. And the same thing happened like in Dubno. Ostrozac was a little town. They surrounded Ostrozac, Ukrainain. And we know what happen when they surround it. Next day, they going to death.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I saw that. I say, Indja, let's go from here. Let's go from here. We ran out from that ghetto. That was a little ghetto. Run out in the woods. It was a little wood for us, run out. Lots of people, lots of Jewish people was in the forest. They thought they cannot survive in the woods. Nothing. So from that point, we run out and we're outside. Outside this. And I say we were sitting in the woods about a day, a day before they killed Ostrozeci people, you know. So now, we're going to go where? We're closer to the east, where the partisans are. So from Ostrozac to Klevan for us I would say about 35 kilometer. 35 kilometer. How we can make 35 kilometer?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- the ground was so wet. And over there is the ground, in that area, was so muddy, muddy. In fact, where we go out on the ground, in the field, we couldn't-- we had a hard time to take out the legs. Walk. Walk. Walk. Very, very hard time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we have to do it. Only me and him. Nobody comes with us. We couldn't take nobody. Old people, we couldn't take it. Women we couldn't take it with us. We would be dead and they would be dead. So by ourselves. Only we both. So that was about 6 o'clock in the evening. We start to walk from one wood, from one forest to the other forest. Was 35 kilometer. The 35 kilometer, we made it in three days. One time, we couldn't make it.

speaker: interviewee
text: The last 10 kilometer, we almost was in the big woods near us. They called us Stadazokuw and Novazokuw. Old Zukow, the-- the village, Zukow. Zukow. Zukow. We went there. The last 10 kilometer, we couldn't make it. We were so hot. We were so hot. I mean, we were hot. And we couldn't make it. Five kilometer from the wood, we saw the wood, the forest, from afar. We [INAUDIBLE], but we made it. We made it. Me and him we made it. One pulled the other one. One pulled the other one. And we couldn't make it. We have to make it before the sun comes out. The sound comes out, and we made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: We came to-- to the other woods. Where are we going? Where are we going? We saw a [NON-ENGLISH]-- a well with water. So we run to that well, and we pulled out the water.

speaker: interviewee
text: We pulled out the water from this, and we start to drink. And then next to the well was a little house, that was about 15 policemans, Ukrainish policemans. See, they know that in the woods are some hiding Jewish people. They were sitting in that little house and as soon as we start to drink the water, we're all surrounded with police. Me and him. Me and Indja Benderski. Surrounded. See, one way we could run-- we was-- we have no shoes-- excuse me-- no shoes, nothing, hungry, thirsty, wet. We couldn't recognize each other. And they started surrounded us, and we run. We run to the woods. He run faster than me did. Indja Benderski run faster me and they caught me. They-- the policeman, they caught me. And they didn't hurt me. Nothing. And they took me, and Indja Benderski run away. He run away and they caught me. They caught me right away. They couldn't kill me. If they would kill me right away, they wouldn't receive a [INAUDIBLE] sugar, or [INAUDIBLE] salt. They wouldn't have the prizes. So they have to take me to the city Klevan. First, they took me to Zukow, to that-- to that village. And that was in the morning. That was about 3 o'clock in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: They took me. They laid me down. They put me on my hands. And they this and that. And then they sometimes an Ukraini came in and kicked me in the this, and kick me here, kick me there, but I didn't pay attention for that. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And one guy, an Ukraini, it was a [UKRAINIAN]. He was a very [UKRAINIAN] man, he belong-- he [UKRAINIAN] this mean he's a very--

speaker: interviewer
text: Religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: A religious person. And he was a policeman too. So he saw right away that nobody looks, he took a piece of bread. He came over and did everything, and he gave it to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what can I do? I ate it. In the morning, they took me on a wagon with a pair horses, and they took me to Klevan to-- to-- to receive the prizes. So they took me. That was about, I would say, about 15, 16 kilometer from there. And they took me over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And two Germans sitting in the front end office. That was a big house. In one side, was living a shoemaker that was working for him. And they was living upstairs in the house. And the downstairs from that big house was the office for the Ukrainish police. They took me there. They took me, and they brought me about 4 o'clock, 5 o'clock in the evening to two Germans. The Germans' names was Ere Schupper. Now, later on I find out what the name of the-- Ere Schupper and Adolph Hammen. Ere Schupper was trade, a baker. He was from Breslau. Wroclaw. Polish is Wroclaw. In German is Breslau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Adolph Hammen was not [GERMAN] not far from Waldenburg. Over there was a [GERMAN]. [BACKGROUND NOISE] He used to work in a [GERMAN] from a-- what you call this is [GERMAN] you know? Sugar.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sugar. A sugar [GERMAN]. I know the addresses because I used to send packages for the Borg families later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was standing in the front of the two Germans. He looked at me, like a strong man. I always used to have red cheeks. [BACKGROUND NOISE] Strong, but dirty and hungry. The first word, the first question he ask me, what is your name? I told him my name. From where you come from? I say, I come from Pulawy. From Lublin, from Pulawy. What [GERMAN]? What kind trade you have? I said, I'm a saddlemaker and I'm a- I'm a-- and I'm a upholsterer. You a saddlemaker. [SPEAKING GERMAN] I talk a little bit German, because I used to learn German in Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he come to me. He said, in German, [GERMAN]. You going to live. But I don't believe him. He told to his girlfriend to give me-- to give me some-- a piece of soap. I take a bath. I clean up myself a little bit. He told her to give me some food. She gave me some food from yesterday. Leftovers. That's good. That's better than good.

speaker: interviewer
text: And next they go, rest a little bit. I say, where I'm going to rest? [BACKGROUND NOISE] I say here in office. I rested there.

speaker: interviewee
text: During the night, all the police was waiting for me. They thought that I'm going to run away from that office. I didn't run. And I didn't believe it. Schupper. I didn't believe him. He just told me. I say, we have lots of-- lots of horses. We need a man like you. [BACKGROUND NOISE] I say, I'm going to give you next day, in the morning. He gave me a big room. Big room. Maybe 20 feet by 40 feet of a room, an empty room. He said, this is going to be your room. And I'm going to give you leathers. Leather. They have lots of leather. The best leather, because they killed already the town, Olyka. Over there is a little town, Olyka. There used to be a lots of saddlemakers. And there used to be lots of leather-- they used to make leather from hides. Leather. And they used to have lots of leather. They gave me a bunch of leather.

speaker: interviewer
text: I say, I want--

speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] --for mine horses-- oh, excuse me. For mine horses, I want-- you should make me two pair of [GERMAN] the three-- for the horses. To pull the horses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Harness?

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, you give me the leather. You give me the everything. All of this. I give you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they gave it to me. All the leather they put in the leather. And they gave me all the [GERMAN]. They call this the-- How they call this? Cinch. The-- for the horse?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- what you call this? [GERMAN] Anyway, all this stuff, they gave it to me, and I start to work right away. They gave me a little-- a table, a chair to sew. And the first day he came in. He was sitting an hour the way I sew. And he saw that I'm a good mechanic, a good saddlemaker, and I don't waste leather, and I don't-- you know, little by little, Mr. Schupper and Hamman, they was to me like two brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I still didn't believe him. When I have-- when I'm going to have a chance, I'm going to run out, because in this side first office, where I was working, in the other side of the rail, where they-- how do you call this? The railing with the-- the railing goes. The-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And it was the woods. I mean, the other side of the road-- The fence?

speaker: interviewee
text: --was the woods. And I-- I-- I-- I just want a chance to run out from here. But they-- every minute, every minute, every second, they looking for me. I mean, they-- they watched me. They watched me. They watched me. They watched me. But I saw they watched me. I saw they watched me. But [INAUDIBLE] But I work for them seven months. Seven months. And the two guys, the two Germans, they was to me couldn't be better. Couldn't be better. Even they Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: They was-- I don't believe they was SS men. They was workers. And I told him all the stories from my-- from my-- from my father, from my family, and they had pity for me. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I say, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. You going to live. Even the Russian will come here, we'll take you with me. We'll take you. We'll take you with me. And that's-- so when the Russians, they start to come closer to Klevan, the Russians pushed the Germans back. They have to-- They have to go out from Klevan. So I went with them. They took me. They gave me a pair horses. And they gave me-- I loaded up the wagon with all kind stuff for them. Not for me, for them. And they start to go out from Klevan. We went from Klevan, we went to Rovno. Rovno was the biggest town in that area. Bigger than Klevan. And from Rovno we went south. We supposed to go to Lwow, Lundberg. That's the route. From Rovno to Lwow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went with them. And I picked the horses. I went with them. And lots of Polish policemen, they-- in that time, Ukraini they killed the Polacks like they killed the Jews. They didn't like the Polacks, because in Ukraine some of the people, they used to marry to Ukraine. They killed Ukraini and the woman they didn't kill, because she was Polish. So that was a group of-- of-- of policemen. And they tried to organize the partisan. So I always was with them. Whatever happen, whatever. They will do it. I go with them. So we went there from Rovno to Lwow.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the road was a little-- a little town. That wasn't a little town. That was a big town, Brody. Brody. And from Brody about [GERMAN]-- 10 kilometer from Brody, we were standing. They had to stay there a few days. A few days and later on, we'll go again to Lwow, south. So we was there about-- I would say, about eight days or nine days.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year is this? 1942? What year is this?

speaker: interviewer
text: Still 1941?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was in 1942. 1942. 1943. That was 1943. See? And we went there. We were standing there in that village. A big village, [NON-ENGLISH]. They called this [NON-ENGLISH]. And we were standing there. And I served the two Germans, Whatever they told me-- to keep clean the horses. We go here, go there.

speaker: interviewer
text: If somebody squeal on me, and they said [BACKGROUND NOISE] that you have Jewish people here with you. Someone Ukraini, they saw me that I'm a Jewish. I say, I'm not Jewish. I have Polish document. What are you talking about? I say no. They went to Brody. And they squeal, or would they talk to-- to the main SS in [INAUDIBLE] that 10 kilometer from here two Germans keeping a Jew. So right away, right away, about two hours later, the whole town, the whole village was surrounded. They want to find me. Where? Where the Jew? See?

speaker: interviewee
text: Luckily, I was in a-- the farmers, they're keeping the hay and this in a--

speaker: interviewer
text: The barn stalls?

speaker: interviewee
text: --in a barn, you know? I was there. And I was in there. And this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a guy came over. Says to me, [INAUDIBLE], you know what? The whole town, the whole village is occupied-- I mean, surrounded. They're looking for you. [SPEAKING GERMAN] I mean, disappear. Disappear from here.

speaker: interviewee
text: From there, I didn't go to the front. I went to the back, to the bar. You know, I went on the field. And I run. I run to the field, to that road was going from [INAUDIBLE] to Lindberg. That was about 2 kilometer from the road.

speaker: interviewer
text: I say, where I'm going to go now? Where I'm going to go now? Just dig a ditch and put yourself in there. And that was about 5 o'clock in the evening. It was dark already. And I said, where I'm going to go? Back, I can't go, because they're looking for me. Here I can go-- where I'm going to go? On the road, there's lots Ukrainish and Germans. They running with the trucks from [? Brody ?] to Lindberg, Lwow to Lindberg, that was the road. The front was not far from there already, about 30 or 40 kilometer. I was laying in that field in a roof over there to 11 o'clock. And I was thinking, what I'm going to do now? Where I'm going to go? Where I'm going to go? So trucks, German trucks, they-- they run back and forth from [? Brody ?] to Lwow. Lwow, [? Brody, ?] [? Brody, ?] Lwow. That was the main road that's going. They-- they-- they said, [INAUDIBLE], that they run away already from the front, because the Russians, they came to us. You know what I did? I went on the front of the-- of the-- on the road and I hold up a German truck. That was about five, six running. They get to go by themself. Five, six trucks they running from [? Brody ?] to Lwow. [? Brody ?] to Lwow. I hold them up and I said-- they ask me, who are you? I say I'm a Polack from that [INAUDIBLE]. They killed my father, my mother. They killed all the Polacks--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the Ukraini, they killed the Polacks and they used to take blood from the Polacks they put on the Polish, on the doors. [SPEAKING POLISH] With blood they did. With blood. With Polish blood.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they knew what's going on in that area. So they buy that my story. And they took me going in the truck.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went in the truck in the back. They didn't know that I'm Jewish. And they said, we're not going to Lwow. We'd going to Podhorce. Podhorce is about 10 kilometer from that place where they took me. Podhorce. A little town. In a little town, Podhorce over there used to be-- King Sobieski. The Polish King Sobieski, he used to live in a-- in a castle. And from that castle, they make a [INAUDIBLE]--

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, before this, I talked to the Germans, where I'm going to sleep? We're going to Podhorce not to Lwow. We're not going to Lwow. We're going Podhorce. I said, I want to go out from that area. So let's meet Podhorce. So I went to Podhorce. They gave me a saddle-- a riding to the main men from the-- [NON-ENGLISH] from that village to give me where to sleep, because I'm a Polack. So that guy, he didn't look too much of me. He say I take with that this. I give it to him. He say, OK, I'll give you a place where to sleep. So he put me in a little store-- in a little room. And I slept already. And about 2 o'clock in the morning, 3 o'clock of morning, I say it's no good. He's going to look at my face. He going to see that I'm a Jewish. I'm a Jew. [INAUDIBLE]. So I opened a window, slowly. That was a hilly, hilly there. I went down. I took my gun. I always used to have a gun in my pant. Always, in case somebody's going kill me, I kill myself. So I said, it's no good to have a gun with me now. See? And I throw away the gun in the bushes. And I went out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm going slowly, slowly. And I went-- I went to that place. I went to that house where it's closer to the woods, where the Polish partisan are. It was a group of partisans, Polacks. They run away from the police. They don't want to be together with Ukrainish police.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I know approximately where the are. So I went closer to that woods. But I couldn't go, because that was about three kilometer from that little town, from Podhorce through the woods. So I went into the house. And I saw lots of people is in that house. Lots of Polacks. They killed the fathers and the mothers and the kids. They run away to that house. They were sitting there in the end of Podhorce. In the end of that little town, Podhorce. I was there. I was there. I say, in the nighttime, I go to the woods and I go-- and I will be with my parents-- with my people, with my boys.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there from 8 o'clock in the morning. About two o'clock, somebody recognized me, that I'm a Jewish. Between the-- maybe that was 150 people in that room. I didn't know that.

speaker: interviewer
text: In one second, they surround me, a few police. They didn't see that are police. The private-- anyway, the came and says, you arrested. I say, for what? For what? I say, you Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, I'm not Jewish. I take off my document. I say, I'm not Jewish. I'm a Polack. I'm a Polack.

speaker: interviewer
text: I say, you not a Polack. You're going to tell me that you a Polack? I'm Ukraini. See, Ukrainish people, Polacks people, they can recognize right away.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, no, you come with me. So they took me from that room. They took me from that room to the Podhorce, to the Germans. They took me. And I was standing. And they took me to the office. Two Germans, they were sitting in the front of me. And I was standing straight.

speaker: interviewer
text: I says, what's your name? I says, so so. Under what, what-- I don't remember what. I had many of them. I says, why do you say that you're not Jewish? You Jewish, they said. I said, take off the pants. In the back, were standing two Ukrainish people, two Ukrainish police with rifles. I says, I have a document. I say, where'd he get that document? Where do you get that document? I said, this is the right document for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, no. They winked at to the police. They took the rifles, and they hit me right here. They were standing. I didn't know what they're going to hit me. They hit me right here on one side, one side with the rifles and I fell down. Almost I fainted. I lay on the floor. And the German said, give him a little bit water. We're not going to kill him. We're not going to hang him today. We're going to hang him tomorrow. But I understand German. I understand German. So the police, they gave me a little bit water to wake me up. And they said-- in German, to them, they said, put him in in that jail. Sobieski, he had over there-- the King Sobieski, the Polish King Sobieski, he had a castle there. From that castle, they made that to a jail. They put me in the jail.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they put me in the jail. And I saw where they taking me. Always in my mind, where they take me? Where I am? I want to know where I am. In case I have to run, I'm going to run.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took me in that castle. They opened the big, big doors. And they put me in. And they put me in to the-- to the left. And they open the door. And they kicked me in. And I felled in. I felled in.
